questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 2.1 Revision on Linear Equation in One Variable
questions.question: The length of a leg of an isosceles triangle is 6 meters more than the base. The perimeter is 54 meters. What is the length of the base?
questions.explanation: Equation: \(a + 2(a+6) = 54 \rightarrow a + 2a + 12 = 54 \rightarrow 3a = 42 \rightarrow a = 14\). Option C correct. Option D results from \(a + 2a + 6 = 54\) (forgetting to double the 6).

source_trace.section_anchor: 2.1 Revision on Linear Equation in One Variable

explanations_tiered.definition: Perimeter of a triangle is the sum of its three sides.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Let base = a, each leg = a+6. Perimeter: a + (a+6) + (a+6) = 54 → 3a+12=54 → a=14.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Add up all sides: base + leg + leg = 54. Leg is base+6, so solve for base.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Linear model yields unique base length; legs are 20 m each, check: 14+20+20=54.
worked_solution: Step 1: Define a = base, leg = a+6.
reasoning_steps: Express other sides in terms of base.
misconceptions: Writing only one leg: a + (a+6) = 54.
Forgetting that there are two equal legs.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Isosceles: two equal sides. Perimeter = sum of all sides.

glossary_entry.definition: Triangle with at least two equal sides.

flashcard.front: Isosceles triangle: leg = base+6, perimeter 54 m. Base length?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Isosceles triangle perimeter problem: leg=base+6, P=54 m, base=?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Draw diagram: label base a, legs a+6.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Model geometric problems with linear equations
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Solving gives base = 14 m.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 2.2 Systems of Linear Equations in Two Variables
questions.subsection: 2.2.1 Solving systems of linear equations in two variables using tables
questions.question: The ordered pair \((5,1)\) is checked in a system. It satisfies the first equation but not the second. What does this mean?

options.C: The system has infinitely many solutions
options.D: \((5,1)\) is not a solution to the system

questions.hint: A solution must satisfy both equations simultaneously.
questions.explanation: For an ordered pair to be a solution, it must satisfy every equation in the system. Failing the second equation means it is not a solution. Option D correct.

source_trace.section_anchor: 2.2 Systems of Linear Equations in Two Variables
source_trace.subsection_anchor: 2.2.1 Solving systems of linear equations in two variables using tables
source_trace.content_anchor: Check whether (5,1) is a solution... substitute into both equations

explanations_tiered.definition: A solution to a system of equations must satisfy all equations in the system simultaneously.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Failing even one equation disqualifies the ordered pair from being a solution to the system.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think of it like a team: all players must agree. If one equation says 'no', it's not a solution.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: A candidate ordered pair must make every equation true. If it fails any, it is not a solution to the system.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The solution set is the intersection of the solution sets of individual equations; failing one excludes the point from the intersection.
reasoning_steps: Substitute ordered pair into first equation.
If both are true, it is a solution; otherwise, it is not.
misconceptions: Assuming satisfying one equation is enough.
Thinking the system has no solution because one pair fails (other pairs might work).

glossary_entry.definition: Ordered pair satisfying all equations in the system.

flashcard.front: If (5,1) satisfies first but not second equation, is it a solution?
flashcard.back: No, it must satisfy all equations.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Does (5,1) satisfy first but not second equation count as a system solution?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasise intersection concept with Venn diagram.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer D. A solution must satisfy every equation.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 2.2 Systems of Linear Equations in Two Variables
questions.subsection: 2.2.3 Solving systems of linear equations in two variables by Elimination method
questions.question: Solve using elimination: \(\begin{cases} 2x - 3y = 8 \\ 2x - 10 = 3y \end{cases}\). What is the nature of this system?
questions.explanation: Both equations give \(2x - 3y = 8\) and \(2x - 3y = 10\), which are contradictory. Thus, no solution. Option B correct. Option D would require identical equations.

source_trace.section_anchor: 2.2 Systems of Linear Equations in Two Variables
source_trace.subsection_anchor: 2.2.3 Solving systems of linear equations in two variables by Elimination method

explanations_tiered.definition: An inconsistent system has no solution; equations represent parallel lines.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Rewrite second as \(2x - 3y = 10\). First is \(2x - 3y = 8\). Left sides equal, constants differ → contradiction.
explanations_tiered.beginner: After rewriting, both equations say \(2x-3y\) equals two different numbers, which is impossible. No solution.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Both equations reduce to \(2x-3y = 8\) and \(2x-3y = 10\). Since \(8 \neq 10\), the system has no solution.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The system is inconsistent because the two equations represent parallel lines with different y-intercepts, never intersecting.
reasoning_steps: Write both equations in standard form \(Ax+By=C\).
If \(A,B\) same but \(C\) different, no solution.
misconceptions: Thinking same left side means infinite solutions (requires same right side).
Solving incorrectly by elimination and missing contradiction.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Same slope, different intercept → no solution.

glossary_entry.definition: System with no solution; parallel lines.

publishing_metadata.revision_note: Same left side, different right → no solution.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Rewrite second equation in standard form before comparing.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Contradiction shows no solution.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Solve \(|5 - 3x| = 7\). What is the solution set?
questions.explanation: Case 1: \(5-3x = 7 \rightarrow -3x = 2 \rightarrow x = -\frac{2}{3}\). Case 2: \(5-3x = -7 \rightarrow -3x = -12 \rightarrow x = 4\). Solutions: \(4\) and \(-\frac{2}{3}\). Option D correct.

explanations_tiered.definition: Absolute value equation \(|A| = p\) with \(p>0\) gives two linear equations: \(A = p\) and \(A = -p\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: The distance from 5-3x to 0 is 7, so 5-3x could be 7 or -7. Solve both.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Both solutions satisfy the original equation; no extraneous roots because RHS positive.
misconceptions: Incorrect sign when moving terms: \(5-3x = 7 \rightarrow -3x = 2\) (correct).
Forgetting negative case.

glossary_entry.definition: Equation containing absolute value, solved by cases.

visual_system.visual_description: Number line showing points at x = -2/3 and x = 4, each marked with a filled dot.
visual_system.caption: The equation has two solutions on the real line.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Number line with points at -0.667 and 4 labelled as solutions.

publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer D. Solving gives x=4 and x=-2/3.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Solve \(x^2 + x - 6 = 0\) by factoring. What are the roots?
questions.hint: Find two numbers with product \(-6\) and sum \(1\): \(3\) and \(-2\).
questions.explanation: Factors: \((x+3)(x-2)=0 \rightarrow x = -3\) and \(x = 2\). Option A lists \(2, -3\). Option B reverses signs incorrectly.

explanations_tiered.definition: Factoring transforms quadratic into product of binomials; zero-product property gives roots.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Find numbers that multiply to \(-6\) and add to \(1\): \(3\) and \(-2\). Thus \((x+3)(x-2)=0\), roots \(-3, 2\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: What two numbers multiply to \(-6\) and add to \(1\)? 3 and -2. So factors are (x+3)(x-2)=0.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Factor: \(x^2 + x - 6 = (x+3)(x-2)=0\). Setting each factor zero gives \(x = -3\) and \(x = 2\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The quadratic's roots are the x-intercepts: \(-3\) and \(2\), as the parabola crosses the x-axis at these points.
worked_solution: Step 2: Find pair with sum \(1\): \(3\) and \(-2\).
reasoning_steps: Find two numbers with product \(c\) and sum \(b\).
misconceptions: Sign errors: writing (x-3)(x+2)=0 giving roots 3 and -2 instead.
Listing roots as -2 and 3 (sign reversal).

glossary_entry.definition: Writing quadratic as product of binomials.

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Practice with different signs to avoid errors.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Solve \(4x^2 + 4x + 1 = 0\). What is the solution?
questions.explanation: \(4x^2+4x+1 = (2x+1)^2 = 0 \rightarrow 2x+1=0 \rightarrow x = -\frac{1}{2}\). Option C correct. Option A would come from \(4x^2-1=0\) or sign error.

explanations_tiered.definition: A perfect square trinomial factors as \((ax+b)^2\), giving one repeated root.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Recognise \(4x^2+4x+1 = (2x+1)^2\). Setting equal zero yields \(2x+1=0\), so \(x = -1/2\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: This is a perfect square: \((2x+1)^2 = 0\). The only way a square is zero is if the inside is zero.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Discriminant \(D = 16-16=0\), confirming a repeated root at the vertex.
reasoning_steps: Check if quadratic is perfect square: \((\sqrt{4x^2}) = 2x\), constant = 1, middle term = 2(2x)(1)=4x.
misconceptions: Taking square root incorrectly: \(2x+1 = 0\) is correct; forgetting the ± sign because square is already zero.
Factoring as \((2x-1)^2\) gives positive 1/2.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Solve \(2x^2 + 5x + 3 = 0\) by completing the square. What is the solution set?
questions.hint: Divide by 2: \(x^2 + \frac{5}{2}x + \frac{3}{2} = 0\), then complete square: add \((5/4)^2 = 25/16\).
questions.explanation: Completing square yields \((x + 5/4)^2 = 1/16\), so \(x + 5/4 = \pm 1/4 \rightarrow x = -1, -3/2\). Option A correct.

explanations_tiered.definition: Completing the square rewrites quadratic in vertex form, then solves by taking square roots.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Divide by 2, move constant, add \((5/4)^2\) to both sides, factor left as perfect square, solve.
explanations_tiered.beginner: First, divide by 2 so the x^2 coefficient is 1. Then complete the square by adding (half of 5/2)^2.
explanations_tiered.advanced: This method derives the quadratic formula roots; here the rational roots are \(-1\) and \(-3/2\), factoring to \((x+1)(2x+3)=0\).
misconceptions: Forgetting to divide by a first.
Adding (b/2)^2 only to left side.

glossary_entry.definition: Method for a≠1 by dividing first.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Solve quadratic by making perfect square.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Show both completing square and factoring for same equation.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Solving gives x=-1 and x=-1.5.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Use the quadratic formula to solve \(x^2 + 3x + 1 = 0\). What are the roots?
questions.explanation: Discriminant: \(9 - 4(1)(1) = 5\). So \(x = \frac{-3 \pm \sqrt{5}}{2}\). Option A correct. Option C uses \(b^2-4ac=13\) incorrectly.

explanations_tiered.definition: Quadratic formula solves any quadratic, even when factoring fails.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Plug a=1, b=3, c=1 into formula, simplify discriminant \(\sqrt{5}\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Put the numbers into the formula: a=1, b=3, c=1. Calculate inside the square root first.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The irrational roots \(\frac{-3 \pm \sqrt{5}}{2}\) are real and conjugate; discriminant 5 is not a perfect square.
misconceptions: Sign error: \(-b\) becomes -3 (correct) but forgetting negative sign.
Incorrect discriminant: 9 - 4 = 5, not 13.

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Leave answer in simplest radical form.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: For the quadratic \(x^2 + bx + c = 0\), the sum of roots is 10 and product is 16. What are \(b\) and \(c\)?
questions.explanation: For \(x^2 + bx + c = 0\), sum = \(-b = 10 \rightarrow b = -10\), product = \(c = 16\). Option A correct. Option D would give sum = -10, product = 16.

explanations_tiered.definition: Vieta's formulas give sum = -b/a, product = c/a; here a=1.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Sum = -b = 10 → b = -10. Product = c = 16. So equation is \(x^2 -10x +16=0\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: For x^2 + bx + c = 0, the sum of roots is -b, the product is c. Plug numbers: -b=10 → b=-10, c=16.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Using Vieta: \(r_1+r_2 = -b = 10\) and \(r_1r_2 = c = 16\). Hence b = -10, c = 16.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The quadratic with given sum and product is \(x^2 - (sum)x + product = 0\), so \(x^2 -10x +16=0\), confirming b=-10, c=16.
reasoning_steps: Write sum and product formulas for given form.
misconceptions: Using sum = b instead of -b.
Confusing which coefficient corresponds to b vs c.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Find b,c for x^2+bx+c=0 given sum=10, product=16.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Write the quadratic as x^2 -10x +16 = 0 to check.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Find coefficients from sum and product of roots

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Tolosa and Fatima complete a task in 8 hours. Tolosa and Ojulu complete it in 12 hours. All three together take 6 hours. How many hours would Fatima and Ojulu need?
questions.hint: Let rates: T, F, O. Equations: 1/T+1/F=1/8, 1/T+1/O=1/12, 1/T+1/F+1/O=1/6. Solve for 1/F+1/O.

source_trace.content_anchor: Fatima and Ojulu can complete the task in 8 hours

explanations_tiered.definition: Work rate problems use reciprocal of time: rate = 1/time, combined rate = sum of individual rates.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Set up equations, solve for individual rates, then compute combined rate of Fatima and Ojulu, invert to get time.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Find how much each person does in one hour, add rates to find time together.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: From equations: 1/T+1/F+1/O = 1/6 and (1/T+1/F)+(1/T+1/O) = 1/8+1/12 = 5/24. Subtract gives 1/T=1/24, so T=24. Then 1/F=1/12, 1/O=1/24. Their sum 1/F+1/O=1/8 → 8 hours.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Solving the linear system in reciprocals yields individual times; combined time for F and O is harmonic mean related.
worked_solution: Step 1: Define rates T,F,O as fraction of job per hour.
reasoning_steps: Combine requested rates and invert for time.
misconceptions: Adding times directly instead of rates.
Forgetting to take reciprocal when converting time to rate.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Rate = 1/time. Combined rate = sum of rates.

glossary_entry.definition: Problem involving combined work rates; add rates, not times.

flashcard.front: T+F=8h, T+O=12h, T+F+O=6h. Find F+O time.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Work problem: T+F=8h, T+O=12h, all=6h. Time for F+O?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasise converting times to rates first.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Solve work problems using linear equations
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Solving rate equations gives F+O = 8 hours.
